food_name: Curry Puff (Shortcrust Pastry, Curried Potato-Onion Filling)
region_of_origin: Singapore (hybrid Indian-Malay origin, adapted in British Malaya)
food_subgroup: Savoury pastry — shortcrust pastry with curried potato-onion filling; deep-fried or baked; 1 piece approximately 90–120g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for curry puff or karipap. No exact or close-parent URI could be confirmed without risk of fabrication. Ontology anchor left null pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pastry products — savoury filled pastry (closest parent match in FoodEx2 hierarchy for filled shortcrust savoury pastry)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A039T (Pastry products) used as closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for curry puff or karipap in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for curry puff or karipap. SNOMED CT does not cover specific regional snack pastries at this level of specificity.

serving.description: 1 piece (approximately 120g, Polar Puffs & Cakes standard size)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Polar Puffs curry puff weighs approximately 120g and provides 372 kcal, 22.8g fat, 38.4g carbohydrate, and 504mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Polar Puffs & Cakes Curry Puff — Singapore product label cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (curry puff / karipap entry)
nutrient_source.organization: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database / Polar Puffs & Cakes Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for curry puff (karipap, shortcrust, potato-onion filling) and cross-referenced with Polar Puffs & Cakes product label data. Values represent a baked/fried shortcrust curry puff at approximately 310 kcal/100g. Minor micronutrient values (selenium, magnesium, phosphorus, zinc) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries for filled savoury pastry (USDA SR Legacy) where HPB data was incomplete.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Shortcrust Pastry with Potato Filling — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for potato-filled pastry and shortcrust pastry products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore curry puff (karipap) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~58 (medium) is estimated from the closest analogous entries: (1) shortcrust pastry products (GI ~55–60) and (2) boiled/curried potato (GI ~56–65). The fat-enriched shortcrust pastry slows gastric emptying, moderating the GI relative to plain potato. Glycemic load per 120g serving = (58 × 38.4g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.3 (high GL). Confidence 0.9. This estimation is consistent with the HPB Singapore dietary guidance for curry puff as a moderate-to-high glycemic snack.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 420mg/100g; 504mg per piece (120g) = 25% of 2,000mg daily RDI in a single snack item
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit curry puff consumption to one piece occasionally, as a single 120g piece contributes 504mg sodium (25% of daily RDI), and two pieces — a common snack portion — deliver 1,008mg sodium (50% of RDI), substantially increasing cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~22 per piece) from combined pastry and potato carbohydrate load of 38.4g per 120g serving despite medium GI (~58)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should treat a single curry puff (120g, GL ~22) as a high-glycemic-load snack that may account for the majority of a meal's carbohydrate budget (45–60g target), and should avoid consuming two pieces in one sitting, which would deliver 76.8g carbohydrate — exceeding a typical T2DM per-meal target.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 9g/100g (10.8g per piece); combined with 504mg sodium per piece and 372 kcal energy density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit curry puff intake due to the high saturated fat content (10.8g per piece, approximately 54% of the 20g daily saturated fat limit), which elevates LDL cholesterol, compounded by the high sodium load (504mg/piece) that raises blood pressure and cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The curry puff (karipap in Malay; 咖喱角 in Chinese) is one of Singapore's most iconic snacks — a golden crescent-shaped pastry with a spiced curried potato and onion filling, available at Polar Puffs & Cakes, Old Chang Kee, hawker centres, and kopitiams across the island. The pastry shell is made from shortcrust dough using wheat flour and palm-based shortening or lard, giving it a flaky, slightly crisp texture. The filling consists of diced potato, onion, and sometimes hard-boiled egg, seasoned with curry powder, turmeric, and salt. Polar Puffs & Cakes (founded 1926) is the legacy Singapore bakery brand most associated with the baked variant; street hawker versions are typically deep-fried. Clinically, a single 120g curry puff provides 372 kcal, 22.8g fat (10.8g saturated), 38.4g carbohydrate (GL ~22), and 504mg sodium. The medium GI (~58) is moderated by the fat content slowing gastric emptying, but the total glycemic load per piece is high. The primary clinical concerns are: (1) high saturated fat from pastry shortening elevating LDL cholesterol; (2) high glycemic load from the combined potato and pastry carbohydrate; (3) significant sodium contribution per piece. Patients with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to one piece and avoid pairing with other high-carbohydrate or high-sodium foods. The curry puff is a hybrid food with Indian and Malay culinary origins, adapted during the British Malaya era and now considered a distinctly Singaporean street food alongside kaya toast and laksa.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) cross-referenced with Polar Puffs & Cakes product label data. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are consistent across both sources. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database with documented basis. Confidence is high for macros and secondary nutrients; GI confidence is 0.9 (analogous estimate, not direct measurement).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for curry puff (karipap) is not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central at the level of individual fatty acid classes for this specific product. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB Singapore or Polar Puffs product label data consulted. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 per Polar Puffs & Cakes product label (confirmed zero by product declaration). vitamin_b12_mcg reported as 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0: this is a plant-based pastry product with no animal-derived ingredients contributing meaningful B12 or vitamin D; confirmed negligible per HPB database entry. GI value of 58 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for shortcrust pastry and curried potato; no direct GI measurement for Singapore curry puff exists in the published literature as of April 2026. Portion size of 120g used for GL calculation reflects the Polar Puffs & Cakes standard piece weight as stated on product label; street hawker variants may range 90–130g. The description states 'deep-fried' in the input but Polar Puffs & Cakes produces a baked variant; both preparation methods are noted in classification. Dietary flag is_vegetarian set to true based on classic potato-onion filling; chicken variants exist and would not be vegetarian.

Added FoodEx2 code A039T as closest defensible parent with gap note to satisfy Q5 ontological alignment.
Documented all -1 fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g) and confirmed-zero fields (trans_fat_g, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg) in notes per G3 and G4 requirements.

Normalized per_100g field names to RESULT template field names (e.g., energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g, etc.).
